1: The theme of divine image-bearing fulfilled in Christ: Paul calls believers to be "imitators of God as dearly loved children." The capacity to imitate God rests on adoption into the family of the Son — the one who is "the image of the invisible God" (Colossians 1:15) and who said "Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father" (John 14:9). Christ is the ground of the command: believers can imitate God because they have been placed in the Son who perfectly images the Father.
2: A direct fulfillment: "Christ loved us and gave himself up for us as a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God" applies to Christ the Levitical language of the burnt offering — every complete offering ascended to God as a rēach nīchoach (fragrant aroma, Leviticus 1:9, 13, 17), a voluntary gift that secured acceptance before God. Psalms 40:6-8, quoted in Hebrews 10:5-7, explicitly identifies the offering of the body of Christ as the event that replaces the entire sacrificial system. Christ does not merely bring a sacrifice; he is the sacrifice.
3: The theme of covenant holiness grounded in Christ's sanctifying work: the prohibition of sexual immorality and greed among God's holy people echoes Leviticus 19:2 ("be holy as I the Lord your God am holy") and the broader purity demands of the Mosaic covenant. In Ephesians the basis has shifted: it is Christ who sanctifies the church, cleansing her to be holy and blameless (Ephesians 5:26-27). The ethics of holiness are not self-generated but are the outworking of what Christ has already done.
4: A revelation of God: the replacement of obscenity and foolish talk with thanksgiving names the appropriate speech of a people who know what God has done in Christ. Paul's dominant vocabulary throughout the letter is eucharistic — the gospel creates gratitude (Ephesians 1:3-14; Ephesians 5:20). The ethics of speech are shaped not by moral effort but by the reality the gospel announces: a sacrifice has been offered and accepted; thanksgiving is the only fitting response.
5: A direct Christological claim of eschatological weight: "no immoral, impure or greedy person — such a person is an idolater — has any inheritance in the kingdom of Christ and of God." The unusual phrase "kingdom of Christ and of God" coordinates Christ and God as joint sovereigns over the coming inheritance — an implicit claim about Christ's participation in divine rule (1 Corinthians 15:24-28; Revelation 11:15). The inheritance that was Israel's land-promise is now the eschatological kingdom that only Christ can grant.
6: The theme of Christ's cross as the one event that absorbs God's wrath: "God's wrath comes on those who are disobedient" names the default trajectory from which the cross rescues. Paul's parallel in Romans 5:9 is explicit — "we shall be saved from God's wrath through him." The present tense ("comes") signals an already-active wrath; the gospel is not a precaution against a future threat but an escape from an existing one, accomplished at the cross.
7: The theme of the two-kingdom separation effected by Christ: "do not be partners with them" applies to believers the separation Christ accomplished between the domain of light and the domain of darkness (Ephesians 5:8; Colossians 1:13 — "rescued us from the dominion of darkness and brought us into the kingdom of the Son"). The call to non-participation is not moral fastidiousness but the practical consequence of having been transferred from one realm to another.
8: A direct Christological claim: "you were once darkness, but now you are light in the Lord." Christ is "the light of the world" (John 8:12), and the transformation Paul describes — from darkness to light — is the specific fulfillment of Isaiah 9:2 that Matthew 4:15-16 applies to Jesus's Galilean ministry. The phrase "in the Lord" is decisive: the change of state is not moral improvement but a change of location — being transferred into the One who is light.
9: The theme of the Spirit reproducing Christ's character: "the fruit of the light consists in all goodness, righteousness and truth" describes qualities that are simultaneously ethical requirements and descriptions of Christ's own character — the one who is "holy, righteous and true" (Revelation 3:7; Ephesians 16:5). The Galatians parallel (fruit of the Spirit, Galatians 5:22-23) confirms that these are not self-cultivated virtues but the harvest of Christ's Spirit at work in the new-covenant community.
10: A revelation of God: "find out what pleases the Lord" names Christ as the Lord whose will now governs all of life — replacing the Mosaic law as the moral reference point (see Ephesians 2:15; Ephesians 4:20-21). The new-covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:34 — "they shall all know me" — is fulfilled through the Spirit who teaches what Christ requires (John 14:26; Ephesians 16:13). Moral discernment is not primarily rational but relational: knowing Christ determines what pleases him.
11: The theme of Christ as the light that exposes what darkness conceals: "expose the fruitless deeds of darkness" extends the light-metaphor Christologically. John 3:20-21 identifies Jesus as the Light before whom every hidden deed is disclosed; John 1:5 — "the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it." The believer's call to expose darkness is a participation in Christ's own ministry of illumination.
12: A revelation of God: the shame attached to what the disobedient do in secret measures their distance from the holiness of God made visible in Christ. The verse does not require comment but functions as a negative pointer: sin hides from light because light is Christ's domain, and shame marks every action that cannot bear his presence. The same logic underlies 1 John 1:5-7 — walking in the light means living as if Christ's character is the standard by which all actions are assessed.
13: The theme of Christ as the light in whom all things are made manifest: "everything exposed by the light becomes visible — and everything that is illuminated becomes a light." John 1:4-5 identifies the light with the life of Christ; John 3:21 describes coming to the light as the movement of those who do truth. The principle at work is Christological: the light of Christ's character and resurrection is the only force that can reveal the true nature of what lies in darkness.
14: A direct Christological application: the early Christian hymn fragment — "Wake up, sleeper, rise from the dead, and Christ will shine on you" — applies resurrection language to believers by grounding it in Christ's own resurrection. Isaiah 60:1 ("Arise, shine, for your light has come") and Isaiah 26:19 ("your dead will live; their bodies will rise") provide the OT register; Isaiah 9:2 identifies this arising with the dawning of light on those in the shadow of death. The hymn declares that Christ's resurrection is the force that awakens the spiritually dead — his shining is the cause, not the result, of the awakening.
15: The theme of wisdom fulfilled in Christ: the contrast between unwise and wise recalls Proverbs 1:7 and the Wisdom literature's entire project — the search for the way of life that conforms to God's ordering of creation. Paul identifies Christ explicitly as "the wisdom of God" (1 Corinthians 1:24, 30) and "in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3). Walking wisely is not about prudential calculation but about ordering life according to the One in whom wisdom is embodied.
16: The theme of Christ as the redeemer of time: "redeeming the time, because the days are evil" (exagorazomenoi ton kairon) uses the same redemption vocabulary as Galatians 4:4-5 — "God sent his Son... to redeem those under the law." The "evil days" of the present age are the domain of "the ruler of the kingdom of the air" (Ephesians 2:2); Christ has broken that dominion, and believers participate in the eschatological purchase of time from the power that squanders it.
17: A revelation of God: "understand what the Lord's will is" — where the "Lord" is Christ — names Christ as the source of the moral intelligence that replaces foolishness. The promise of Colossians 1:9 ("filled with the knowledge of his will through all spiritual wisdom") and the identification of Christ as "the way and the truth" (John 14:6) establish that understanding the Lord's will is inseparable from knowing the Lord himself. Discernment is Christocentric.
18: A direct Christological claim: Spirit-filling is the fruit of Christ's ascension. John 7:39 — "the Spirit had not yet been given, since Jesus had not yet been glorified" — makes the connection explicit. Acts 2:33 — "exalted to the right hand of God... he has received from the Father the promised Holy Spirit and has poured out what you now see and hear." To be filled with the Spirit is to receive the gift that only the ascended Christ can give; the command presupposes what Christ has accomplished.
19: The theme of the Psalter fulfilled in Christ: the community is to speak "psalms, hymns, and songs from the Spirit... to the Lord." The OT Psalter supplied the liturgical vocabulary of Israel — and many psalms are read in the NT as pointing forward to Christ (Ps 22 at the crucifixion; Ps 110 at the exaltation; Ps 2 at the resurrection). Singing "to the Lord" — where the Lord is the risen Christ — means the entire hymnody of Israel is now reoriented toward its fulfillment and focus.
20: A direct Christological claim: "always giving thanks to God the Father for everything, in the name of our Lord Jesus Christ." The phrase "in the name" places Christ as the mediator through whom all worship reaches the Father — the practical outworking of Ephesians 2:18 ("through him we both have access to the Father by one Spirit") and Ephesians 3:12. John 14:13-14 grounds this: "whatever you ask in my name, I will do it." Thanksgiving itself is an act of Christological mediation.
21: A revelation of God: mutual submission flows from "reverence for Christ" (en phobō Christou). The Christ who "did not consider equality with God something to be used to his own advantage" and "made himself nothing" (Philippians 2:6-7) is the model for every act of voluntary deference. The entire household code that follows (Ephesians 5:22–Ephesians 6:9) is grounded in this single verse: every relationship is governed not by social convention but by the pattern Christ established in his own self-emptying.
22: A shadow of the Christ-church relationship applied domestically: "submit to your own husbands as to the Lord" does not make husbands lords; it maps the wife's submission onto the church's submission to Christ (v24). The shadow runs from the Christ-church reality to the marriage pattern — Christ-church is primary; husband-wife derives its structure from it. The wife submits not because of her husband's authority in isolation but because the marriage relationship is a type of the church's relationship to Christ.
23: A direct Christological claim: "Christ is the head of the church, his body, of which he is the Savior." This is the foundational datum Paul has stated in 1:22 and Ephesians 4:15; here it governs domestic ethics. The threefold description — head, body, Savior — reflects the cosmic Christology of chapters 1–4 applied to the marriage analogy. The husband-head pattern is a derived application of the Christ-head reality, not its theological equal; the Christological statement is primary.
24: A revelation of God: the church's submission to Christ is the theological norm from which household ethics is derived. The phrase "in everything" applied to the church's submission to Christ is absolute — there is no domain of life outside Christ's lordship. Paul applies this norm to marriage with "so also" — the domestic relationship participates in the structure of the Christ-church relationship, not the reverse. The order of statement is theologically deliberate.
25: A direct Christological claim and the most explicit christologizing of domestic ethics in the NT: "husbands, love your wives, just as Christ loved the church and gave himself up for her." The cross is the norm for spousal love — not affection, not eros, but the self-expenditure of Gethsemane and Golgotha. The Levitical code knew many duties; Paul here identifies a love that has no OT domestic precedent, derived entirely from the character of Christ's act at the cross.
26: A direct Christological claim: Christ "sanctify and cleansed" the church "by the washing with water through the word." The priestly-kingly role of Christ echoes Ezekiel 16:9 (God washing Jerusalem clean) and the Levitical purification rites; Hebrews 9:14 — "how much more will the blood of Christ... cleanse our consciences." The "word" through which the washing occurs is the gospel word about Christ himself — the proclamation that both announces and effects the cleansing it describes.
27: A type pointing to eschatological fulfillment: the presentation of the church as "a radiant bride, without stain or wrinkle or any other blemish, holy and blameless" draws on the bridegroom-bride imagery of Isaiah 62:5 (God rejoicing over Jerusalem as a bridegroom) and the Song of Solomon's beloved. The eschatological trajectory runs from these OT figures through to Revelation 19:7-8 ("the wedding of the Lamb has come, and his bride has made herself ready") and Revelation 21:2 (the new Jerusalem "prepared as a bride"). Christ is the divine bridegroom preparing his people for the eternal marriage.
28: A revelation of God: the call for husbands to love their wives "as their own bodies" is grounded in the union between Christ and the church stated in v30 — "we are members of his body." Because Christ loves the church as his own body (nourishing and cherishing it, v29), the husband's love for his wife as his own body is a participation in the love-structure that governs the primary relationship. The domestic ethic is derivative of the Christological reality.
29: A direct Christological claim: "just as Christ does the church" — Christ nourishes (ektrefei) and cherishes (thalpei) the church as though caring for his own body. These are not abstract descriptions of divine benevolence but a claim about the ongoing relation of the risen Christ to his people. The body of Christ (Ephesians 1:23; Ephesians 4:4, 16) is the living organism Christ perpetually sustains; believers are the beneficiaries of a care that is structurally identical to his care for himself.
30: A direct Christological claim: "we are members of his body" — the body-of-Christ ecclesiology is the foundation on which the marriage analogy rests (vv23-29) and the reason the husband's love for his wife is structurally connected to Christ's love for the church. This membership is not organizational but ontological — union with Christ (John 15:4-5; 1 Corinthians 6:15; Ephesians 12:12-27). The verse is brief because it restates a theme central to the entire letter.
31: A type fulfilled: Paul quotes Genesis 2:24 — "a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh" — as evidence for the union of Christ and the church, which he discloses explicitly in the next verse. The primordial marriage was designed by God as a type of the Christ-church union: the "leaving" of the father anticipates the Son's coming from the Father; the "cleaving" to the wife anticipates the incarnation and cross; the "one flesh" anticipates the church as Christ's own body. The type was embedded in creation at the beginning.
32: A direct typological disclosure: "This is a profound mystery — but I am talking about Christ and the church." This is the most explicit typological statement in Ephesians and one of the most explicit in the NT: Paul announces that the mystery hidden in Genesis 2:24 is the union of Christ and the church. Marriage as an institution was designed from creation to be a sign pointing toward its own anti-type. The "profound mystery" (mystērion mega) is not that marriage is meaningful, but that it was always about Christ.
33: A revelation of God: the domestic application ("let each love his wife... and the wife must respect her husband") brings the cosmic Christology of vv22-32 into the household. The mutual ethic — sacrificial love from the husband, honor from the wife — mirrors in miniature what Christ and the church are to each other: the head who gives himself and the body that responds with reverence. The Christ-church relationship is not an illustration of marriage; marriage is an icon of Christ and the church.
